rationale: Both implementations only add IDs and rely on whole-set expiry, so they enforce distinct users per TTL window rather than concurrent users.
Because SET_KEY is expired before TTL_KEY is written with the same TTL, the marker can remain after SET_KEY expires and suppress expiry on a recreated set.

The Lua script does allow an existing member before checking size, but this is the intended correction for the review's documented lockout and is only a compatibility consideration for pre-existing over-limit sets.

revision.claim: Document that the Lua fix admits existing members when a pre-existing set already exceeds MAX_USERS, as part of correcting the lockout behavior.

rationale: The original path uses five Redis commands only when a new ID finds no TTL marker, while existing IDs use two and normal later new IDs use three.

revision.claim: Five commands occur whenever added == 1 and TTL_KEY is absent, normally the first user after both keys have expired; common existing-user and later-new-user paths use two and three commands respectively.

rationale: The review correctly identifies a failure window that can leave SET_KEY without expiry; possible recovery on a later distinct user does not negate that conditional memory-leak risk.

Neither the original code nor the proposed Lua script contains a remove (SREM) operation, meaning both effectively cap total distinct users over the TTL window rather than currently active concurrent users.
Because SET_KEY is assigned its expiration before TTL_KEY is set, TTL_KEY will expire slightly later, and any SADD occurring in that short window will falsely skip the expiration block.

The Lua script explicitly returns 1 to allow existing members before checking the size limit, whereas the original code evaluated size and rejected requests even for returning users, representing an undocumented behavioral shift.
The five operations only occur when adding a new user and the TTL marker does not exist; returning users require two operations, and new users with an existing marker require three.
The missing TTL is not a permanent leak, as the very next new user will evaluate hasKey(TTL_KEY) as false and consequently re-apply the expiration to the set.

Neither original code nor Lua ever issues SREM; both only SADD, so members accumulate until the whole-set TTL expires, capping distinct users per window rather than tracking concurrency, contradicting the 'ConcurrentUserLimiter' name; the review never flags this.

expire(SET_KEY,ttl) is issued before set(TTL_KEY,ttl), so TTL_KEY's absolute expiry is marginally later; if SET_KEY expires first, a new SADD recreates it while hasKey(TTL_KEY) is still true, skipping expire and leaving the recreated set without TTL until the marker expires.

The Lua's SISMEMBER-then-return-1 does admit existing members before any size check, a genuine and undocumented divergence from the original's reject-all-when-over-capacity, but it is the intended fix for the lockout bug, not itself a defect.

revision.claim: Lua admits existing members before the size check, an undocumented (though arguably desirable) behavior change from the original's reject-all-over-capacity that the review does not call out.

rationale: The five ops (SADD+hasKey+expire+set+SCARD) occur only for the first-ever user; a returning user does 2 (SADD+SCARD) and a new user with the marker present does 3, so 'five to one' is worst case, not steady state.

A crash after SADD before expire also leaves TTL_KEY unwritten, so the next distinct user finds ttlMarkerExists=false and re-issues expire(SET_KEY,ttl), repairing the leak unless all subsequent traffic is from already-present members.
